## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_countries&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Faroese_folk_dance_club_from_vagar.jpg/250px-Faroese_folk_dance_club_from_vagar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Faroese_folk_dance_club_from_vagar.jpg)

Faroese folk dancers in national costumes

Nordic countries have historically had some of the most socially progressive cultures in the world, and culture is one of the main bases of cooperation between them. The policies of the Nordic countries with respect to cultural life, mass media and religion have many shared values and features in common. However, some differences may be seen, for instance in cultural institutions that arise from historical circumstances. In both Denmark and Sweden, there are cultural institutions with roots in the traditions of the royal courts. In these countries, national institutions formed the foundation of cultural life at an early stage while in Norway cultural institutions began to form later.[[135]](#cite_note-136)

Iceland has the highest government expenditure on culture, a total of 3.3 per cent of its GDP in 2011. Denmark comes second with a total of 1.6 per cent of GDP in 2011. Sweden spend the least in 2011 with 1.1 per cent. Looking at per capita expenditure, Iceland again has the highest expenditure with Norway coming second. Greenland spends the third highest amount on culture and leisure per capita. In Iceland and Norway, expenditures have more than doubled since 2000. In the other Nordic countries, expenditures have gone up between 40 and 50 per cent in the same period.[[136]](#cite_note-137)

Denmark has the most museums, a total of 274, but museums in Åland and Iceland have the most visitors, an average of 4 and 5 visits per inhabitant. Many theatres in the Nordic countries receive public funding. Theatre funding constitutes a major share of allocations within the cultural area in all the countries. All countries have national theatres, where plays, ballets and operas are performed. In addition to the national theatres, there are professional regional theatres, which are also supported by the state, counties or municipalities. Most countries also have a few private theatres and many amateur ensembles, which may be supported at least partially by municipalities, primarily.[[137]](#cite_note-138)

The Nordic Culture Fund, established in 1966, aims to support a broad spectrum of cultural cooperations between the Nordic countries. The Fund's ambition is to enable talented artists, both professionals and amateurs, to enrich each other via the cultural diversity that exists among the 26 million or more people of the Region. Its activities are based on an agreement between the Nordic countries, which came into force in 1967. The Fund receives its money in the form of an annual grant from the Nordic Council of Ministers.[[138]](#cite_note-139)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_countries&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Music")]

See also: [Nordic folk music](/wiki/Nordic_folk_music "Nordic folk music") and [Nordic popular music](/wiki/Nordic_popular_music "Nordic popular music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/ABBA_Rotterdam_1979.jpg/250px-ABBA_Rotterdam_1979.jpg)](/wiki/File:ABBA_Rotterdam_1979.jpg)

[ABBA](/wiki/ABBA "ABBA") is one of the [best-selling music artists of all time](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists").

Nordic countries share certain traditions in music, many of which have diverged significantly. In [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), Denmark, Iceland, Norway, Sweden and the Faroe Islands share many common aspects. Greenland's [Inuit culture](/wiki/Inuit_culture "Inuit culture") has its own musical traditions, influenced by Scandinavian culture. Finland shares many cultural similarities with both the other Nordic countries as well as [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"). The Sami have their own unique culture, with ties to the neighboring cultures.

Art music has a strong position in Nordic countries. Apart from state-owned opera houses, there are symphony orchestras in most major cities. The most prominent historical composers from Nordic countries are the Finn [Jean Sibelius](/wiki/Jean_Sibelius "Jean Sibelius"), the Dane [Carl Nielsen](/wiki/Carl_Nielsen "Carl Nielsen") and the Norwegian [Edvard Grieg](/wiki/Edvard_Grieg "Edvard Grieg"). Of contemporary composers, the Finns [Magnus Lindberg](/wiki/Magnus_Lindberg "Magnus Lindberg"), [Kaija Saariaho](/wiki/Kaija_Saariaho "Kaija Saariaho") and [Esa-Pekka Salonen](/wiki/Esa-Pekka_Salonen "Esa-Pekka Salonen") are among the most often performed in the world.

Rock and roll influences that came from the United States and United Kingdom were the start of the Nordic pop scene, but influences from the Nordic folk music can still be found today in popular music. Common characteristic in Nordic pop music is that it can often be either very lighthearted pop music or very dark metal. Some of the most well-known Nordic music groups include [ABBA](/wiki/ABBA "ABBA"), [Ace of Base](/wiki/Ace_of_Base "Ace of Base"), [a-ha](/wiki/A-ha "A-ha"), [Aqua](/wiki/Aqua_(band) "Aqua (band)"), [Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk"), [The Cardigans](/wiki/The_Cardigans "The Cardigans"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe_(band) "Europe (band)"), [Hanoi Rocks](/wiki/Hanoi_Rocks "Hanoi Rocks"), [Roxette](/wiki/Roxette "Roxette"), [The Rasmus](/wiki/The_Rasmus "The Rasmus"), [Kaizers Orchestra](/wiki/Kaizers_Orchestra "Kaizers Orchestra") and [The Spotnicks](/wiki/The_Spotnicks "The Spotnicks"). Sweden and Finland have possibly the largest music industries in the area, especially Sweden which is the largest exporter of pop music per capita and the third largest overall after the United States and the United Kingdom. Norway, Iceland and Denmark have all had successful domestic record industries for many years.[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141)

The Nordic metal scene is highly visible compared to other genres from the region. Many big names such as [Amon Amarth](/wiki/Amon_Amarth "Amon Amarth"), [Children of Bodom](/wiki/Children_of_Bodom "Children of Bodom"), [In Flames](/wiki/In_Flames "In Flames"), [Meshuggah](/wiki/Meshuggah "Meshuggah") and [Opeth](/wiki/Opeth "Opeth") originate from the Nordic countries. Nordic metal bands have had a long and lasting influence on the metal subculture alongside their counterparts in the United Kingdom and the United States. The [black metal](/wiki/Black_metal "Black metal") genre was developed in Norway by bands such as [Mayhem](/wiki/Mayhem_(band) "Mayhem (band)"), [Darkthrone](/wiki/Darkthrone "Darkthrone"), [Burzum](/wiki/Burzum "Burzum"), [Immortal](/wiki/Immortal_(band) "Immortal (band)") and [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_(Norwegian_band) "Emperor (Norwegian band)") and the related genre of [Viking metal](/wiki/Viking_metal "Viking metal") was developed throughout the Nordic region by bands such as [Bathory](/wiki/Bathory_(band) "Bathory (band)"), [Enslaved](/wiki/Enslaved_(band) "Enslaved (band)"), Burzum, Emperor, [Einherjer](/wiki/Einherjer "Einherjer"), [Moonsorrow](/wiki/Moonsorrow "Moonsorrow") and Amon Amarth.

Since 2000, the total sale of music has declined by almost 50 per cent in all the Nordic countries and at the same time digital sales have increased (covering both downloads and streaming). In Denmark, Norway and Finland, the sale of digital music has increased by 400 per cent since 2006 and now amounts to 39, 27 and 25 per cent of the total sale in 2010/2011. In Denmark and Sweden, sales of digital music rose almost eight-fold in the same period and now represent 51 per cent of the total sale. In Iceland, digital sales still only represent three per cent of the total sale.[[141]](#cite_note-142)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_countries&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Literature")]

See also: [Nordic literature](/wiki/Nordic_literature "Nordic literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Kierkegaard.jpg/250px-Kierkegaard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kierkegaard.jpg)

[Søren Kierkegaard](/wiki/S%C3%B8ren_Kierkegaard "Søren Kierkegaard") is considered to be the first existentialist philosopher.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Astrid_Lindgren_and_Tove_Jansson_in_1958.jpg/250px-Astrid_Lindgren_and_Tove_Jansson_in_1958.jpg)](/wiki/File:Astrid_Lindgren_and_Tove_Jansson_in_1958.jpg)

Swedish author [Astrid Lindgren](/wiki/Astrid_Lindgren "Astrid Lindgren") together with Finnish author [Tove Jansson](/wiki/Tove_Jansson "Tove Jansson") in Stockholm in 1958

The earliest written records from Scandinavia are [runic inscriptions](/wiki/Runic_inscriptions "Runic inscriptions") on memorial stones and other objects. Some of those contain allusions to [Norse mythology](/wiki/Norse_mythology "Norse mythology") and even short poems in [alliterative verse](/wiki/Alliterative_verse "Alliterative verse"). The best known example is the elaborate [Rök runestone](/wiki/R%C3%B6k_runestone "Rök runestone") (c. 800) which alludes to legends from the migration age. The oldest of the [Eddic poems](/wiki/Eddic_poems "Eddic poems") are believed to have been composed in the 9th century, though they are only preserved in 13th-century manuscripts. They tell of the myths and heroic legends of Scandinavia. [Skaldic poetry](/wiki/Skaldic_poetry "Skaldic poetry") is mostly preserved in late manuscripts but was preserved orally from the 9th century onwards and also appears on runestones, such as the [Karlevi Runestone](/wiki/Karlevi_Runestone "Karlevi Runestone"). In Iceland the [sagas of Icelanders](/wiki/Sagas_of_Icelanders "Sagas of Icelanders") are the best-known specimens of [Icelandic literature](/wiki/Icelandic_literature "Icelandic literature"). In Finland the most famous collection of folk poetry is by far the [Kalevala](/wiki/Kalevala "Kalevala"), which is the [national epic](/wiki/National_epic "National epic") of the country.

Nordic countries have produced important and influential literature. [Henrik Ibsen](/wiki/Henrik_Ibsen "Henrik Ibsen"), a Norwegian playwright, was largely responsible for the popularity of modern realistic drama in Europe, with plays like *[The Wild Duck](/wiki/The_Wild_Duck "The Wild Duck")* and *[A Doll's House](/wiki/A_Doll%27s_House "A Doll's House")*. His contemporary, Swedish novelist and playwright [August Strindberg](/wiki/August_Strindberg "August Strindberg"), was a forerunner of experimental forms such as [expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism"), [symbolism](/wiki/Symbolism_(arts) "Symbolism (arts)") and [surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"). [Nobel prizes for literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_for_Literature "Nobel Prize for Literature") have been awarded to [Selma Lagerlöf](/wiki/Selma_Lagerl%C3%B6f "Selma Lagerlöf"), [Verner von Heidenstam](/wiki/Verner_von_Heidenstam "Verner von Heidenstam"), [Karl Adolph Gjellerup](/wiki/Karl_Adolph_Gjellerup "Karl Adolph Gjellerup"), [Henrik Pontoppidan](/wiki/Henrik_Pontoppidan "Henrik Pontoppidan"), [Knut Hamsun](/wiki/Knut_Hamsun "Knut Hamsun"), [Sigrid Undset](/wiki/Sigrid_Undset "Sigrid Undset"), [Erik Axel Karlfeldt](/wiki/Erik_Axel_Karlfeldt "Erik Axel Karlfeldt"), [Frans Eemil Sillanpää](/wiki/Frans_Eemil_Sillanp%C3%A4%C3%A4 "Frans Eemil Sillanpää"), [Johannes Vilhelm Jensen](/wiki/Johannes_Vilhelm_Jensen "Johannes Vilhelm Jensen"), [Pär Lagerkvist](/wiki/P%C3%A4r_Lagerkvist "Pär Lagerkvist"), [Halldór Laxness](/wiki/Halld%C3%B3r_Laxness "Halldór Laxness"), [Nelly Sachs](/wiki/Nelly_Sachs "Nelly Sachs"), [Eyvind Johnson](/wiki/Eyvind_Johnson "Eyvind Johnson"), [Harry Martinson](/wiki/Harry_Martinson "Harry Martinson") and [Tomas Tranströmer](/wiki/Tomas_Transtr%C3%B6mer "Tomas Tranströmer"). World-famous Nordic [children's book](/wiki/Children%27s_book "Children's book") writers include [Hans Christian Andersen](/wiki/Hans_Christian_Andersen "Hans Christian Andersen"), [Tove Jansson](/wiki/Tove_Jansson "Tove Jansson") and [Astrid Lindgren](/wiki/Astrid_Lindgren "Astrid Lindgren").

Since 1962, the Nordic Council has awarded a [literature prize](/wiki/Nordic_Council%27s_Literature_Prize "Nordic Council's Literature Prize") once a year for a work of fiction written in one of the Nordic languages. Since its establishment, the prize has been won by fifteen Swedish, ten Danish, ten Norwegian, eight Finnish, seven Icelandic, two Faroe and one Sami writers.[[142]](#cite_note-143)

Nordic libraries function as information centres with a wide variety of services and access to all kinds of printed and electronic media. In the last twenty years, there has been an overall decline in stock and lending of books in public libraries. Despite the general decline in stock and loans, most of the Nordic countries have had an increase in the lending of other media than books. Since 2000, the stock of other media has increased between 30 and 85 per cent. The lending of books has at the same time decreased in all Nordic countries, a decline between 10 and 20 per cent.[[143]](#cite_note-144)

## National symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nordic_countries&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: National symbols")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Nordiska_Radet_session_i_Helsingfors_29.10.2012_%284%29.jpg/250px-Nordiska_Radet_session_i_Helsingfors_29.10.2012_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nordiska_Radet_session_i_Helsingfors_29.10.2012_(4).jpg)

Flags of the Nordic countries, its territories, and the [Nordic Council](/wiki/Nordic_Council "Nordic Council") from left to right: Finland, Åland, Denmark, Faroe Islands, Greenland, Iceland, Norway, Sweden and the Nordic Council

The Nordic countries, including the autonomous territories of the Faroe Islands and Åland, have a similar flag design. They display an off-centre cross with the intersection closer to the hoist – the "[Nordic cross](/wiki/Nordic_cross_flag "Nordic cross flag")" or "Scandinavian cross"[[144]](#cite_note-145)– however each has [a different aspect ratio](/wiki/List_of_aspect_ratios_of_national_flags "List of aspect ratios of national flags"). This cross design was first seen in the [Dannebrog](/wiki/Flag_of_Denmark "Flag of Denmark"), the national flag of Denmark.[[145]](#cite_note-146) [Greenland](/wiki/Flag_of_Greenland "Flag of Greenland") and [Sápmi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_flag "Sámi flag") have adopted flags without the Nordic cross, but they both feature a circle similarly placed off-centre representing the sun setting on the horizon.[[146]](#cite_note-147)
